Sacramento, CA 
95804 
Gentlemen: 
A question has arisen as to the proper treatment of the 
purchase of shares in a municipal bond trust by a political 
campaign committee. 
Your Los Angeles office recommended that such an invest-
ment be treated as an expenditure of the period, thus reducing 
the committee's cash balance. 
wnen the shares are sold, they 
are to be recognized as a miscellaneous increase to cash. 
This 
is the treatment which we have followed in our reports to date. 
The question arises because subsequent reports appear to 
misrepresent the committee's financial position. 
The cash 
balance is the only asset shown on the report. 
The investment 
in the trust does not appear again until the shares are sold, 
although at any point in time they are readily convertible to 
cash. 
We would appreciate your opinion as to which alternative 
treatment of this item is correct for the committee reporting 
purposes. 
In that this situation is similar to the purchase 
of certificates of deposit, we would appreciate an opinion with 
regard to both types of transactions. 
Thank you for your consideration. 
Sincerely,

sections.question: A question has arisen as to the proper treatment of the purchase of shares in a municipal bond trust by a political campaign committee. Your Los Angeles office recommended that such an investment be treated as an expenditure of the period, thus reducing the committee's cash balance. When the shares are sold, they are to be recognized as a miscellaneous increase to cash. This is the treatment which we have followed in our reports to date. The question arises because subsequent reports appear to misrepresent the committee's financial position. The cash balance is the only asset shown on the report. The investment in the trust does not appear again until the shares are sold, although at any point in time they are readily convertible to cash. We would appreciate your opinion as to which alternative treatment of this item is correct for the committee reporting purposes. In that this situation is similar to the purchase of certificates of deposit, we would appreciate an opinion with regard to both types of transactions.
sections.conclusion: The purchase of certificates of deposit, stocks, shares in a municipal bond trust or any other asset that can be readily converted to cash should not be reported as expenditures, nor should the committee's cash balance be reduced by the change in assets. At the time the asset is converted back to cash, any increase or decrease to the asset value should be reported as a miscellaneous adjustment to cash on the committee campaign statement.
sections.question_synthetic: Should a political campaign committee report the purchase of shares in a municipal bond trust (or certificates of deposit) as an expenditure that reduces cash balance, or should such readily convertible assets be handled differently for reporting purposes?
sections.conclusion_synthetic: Assets readily convertible to cash, including municipal bond trust shares and certificates of deposit, should not be reported as expenditures or reduce the cash balance. Instead, gains or losses from converting these assets back to cash should be reported as miscellaneous adjustments to cash at the time of conversion.
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: Document contains both incoming request (February 5, 1980 letter from E.T. Jacobs) and FPPC response (March 7, 1980 letter from J. Richard Eichman). Extracted response sections. No formal QUESTION/CONCLUSION headers present; question and conclusion identified by content context. Minor OCR errors present in original document (e.g., 'conlir.m' for 'confirm', 'asa~-::' for 'asset').

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: A question has arisen as to the proper treatment of the purchase of shares in a municipal bond trust by a political campaign committee. Your Los Angeles office recommended that such an investment be treated as an expenditure of the period, thus reducing the committee's cash balance. When the shares are sold, they are to be recognized as a miscellaneous increase to cash. This is the treatment which we have followed in our reports to date. The question arises because subsequent reports appear to misrepresent the committee's financial position. The cash balance is the only asset shown on the report. The investment in the trust does not appear again until the shares are sold, although at any point in time they are readily convertible to cash. We would appreciate your opinion as to which alternative treatment of this item is correct for the committee reporting purposes. In that this situation is similar to the purchase of certificates of deposit, we would appreciate an opinion with regard to both types of transactions.

CONCLUSION: The purchase of certificates of deposit, stocks, shares in a municipal bond trust or any other asset that can be readily converted to cash should not be reported as expenditures, nor should the committee's cash balance be reduced by the change in assets. At the time the asset is converted back to cash, any increase or decrease to the asset value should be reported as a miscellaneous adjustment to cash on the committee campaign statement.

embedding.summary: The FPPC clarifies that purchases of readily convertible assets like municipal bond trust shares and certificates of deposit should not be treated as expenditures or reduce a political campaign committee's cash balance; rather, any gains or losses should be reported as miscellaneous adjustments when the assets are converted back to cash.
